Lanthanide in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> oxidation state has strong tendency to loss <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> and converted into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> oxidation state therefore act as strong reducing agent. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∴</mo><msub><mi>EuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> act as strong reducing agent.

solution:
Ellingham diagram is a graphical representation of ΔG vs T when metal heated with oxygen to form metal oxide.

An Ellingham diagram is a graph showing the temperature dependence of the stability for compounds. This analysis is usually used to evaluate the ease of reduction of metal oxides and sulphides.

Ellingham diagram represents change in free energy ΔG with temperature

solution:
Given curves, x2a+y2b=1 .....i

On differentiating equation i, we get

2xa+2ybdydx=0

⇒m1=dydx=-xbya ...iii

and x2c+y2d=1.....ii 

On differentiating equation ii, we get

2xc+2yddydx=0

⇒m2=dydx=-xdyc ...iv

For orthogonal curves, m1×m2=-1

⇒-xbya×-xdyc=-1

x2y2=-acbd....v

From equation i-ii, we get

x2c-aac=y2b-dbd

⇒x2y2=b-dc-aacbd

From equation v, we get

 a-c=b-d

⇒ a-b=c-d.

subject: Mathematics
options: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> is onto, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is one-one
If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is one-one, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> is one-one
solution:
fn+1-fn=f1

A.P. with common difference = f(1)

General term = Tn = f(1) + (n-1)f(1) = nf(1)

⇒ fn=nf1⇒f is one-one

Now, Let fgx2=fgx1

⇒ gx2=gx1 (as f is one-one)

⇒ x1=x2 (as fog is one-one)

⇒ g is one-one

For f to be onto, f(n) should take all the values of natural numbers.

As f(x) is increasing, f(1)=1

⇒f(n)=n

Now, fgn=gn f1

may be many-one if

gn is many-one

So “if g is onto, then fog is one-one” is incorrect.

So, direction cosines of two lines are

0,12,12 and 12,0,12

⇒α=π3

∴ sin4α+cos4α=1-12sin2(2α)=1-12·34=58
